coredata.dc:description: In order to develop water splitting as a sustainable technology, cost-effective and green electrocatalysts are urgently needed. We report a fabrication of an efficient self-supported whole-carbon electrocatalyst for overall water splitting in a wide pH range, which consist of nitrogen-doped vertically aligned graphene arrays and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-VG-CQDs) prepared from vinegar residue. N-VG-CQDs electrocatalyst displays excellent electrocatalytic performances towards hydrogen evolution reaction (overpotentials of 218 and 238 mV in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively), as well as outstanding performances towards oxygen evolution reaction (overpotentials of 383 and 497 mV in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively). In addition, N-VG-CQDs acts as a self-supporting bifunctional electrocatalyst, exhibiting good stabilities in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes and an excellent Faraday efficiency (about 83 %). This study sheds light on the usage of vinegar residue towards the development of cost-effective whole-carbon electrocatalysts in water splitting. And the study also facilitates the use of environmental pollutions to prepare electrocatalysts for water splitting and advances the development of the water splitting industry.

head.ce:title: N-doped vertical graphene arrays/carbon quantum dots derived from vinegar residue as efficient water-splitting catalyst in a wide pH range

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to develop water splitting as a sustainable technology, cost-effective and green electrocatalysts are urgently needed. We report a fabrication of an efficient self-supported whole-carbon electrocatalyst for overall water splitting in a wide pH range, which consist of nitrogen-doped vertically aligned graphene arrays and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-VG-CQDs) prepared from vinegar residue. N-VG-CQDs electrocatalyst displays excellent electrocatalytic performances towards hydrogen evolution reaction (overpotentials of 218 and 238 mV in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively), as well as outstanding performances towards oxygen evolution reaction (overpotentials of 383 and 497 mV in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively). In addition, N-VG-CQDs acts as a self-supporting bifunctional electrocatalyst, exhibiting good stabilities in both acidic and alkaline electrolytes and an excellent Faraday efficiency (about 83 %). This study sheds light on the usage of vinegar residue towards the development of cost-effective whole-carbon electrocatalysts in water splitting. And the study also facilitates the use of environmental pollutions to prepare electrocatalysts for water splitting and advances the development of the water splitting industry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There is an urgent need to develop sustainable technologies for producing alternative clean energy fuels [1,2]. Recently, hydrogen energy is widely regarded as promising pollutant-free energy source. Water splitting is considered as a prominent technology for the large scale production of H2 [3]. The process of water splitting includes the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode and the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode. However, producing hydrogen directly by water splitting is energy-consuming. Therefore, catalysts are deemed efficient in reducing the overpotential in hydrogen production by water splitting, accelerating the reaction speed, thus improving the energy conversion efficiency [4]. Currently, noble metals such as Pt/C, IrO2 and Ru are used as catalysts for water splitting due to their excellent catalytic properties. However, the scarcity and high price of noble metals hinder the development of the electrocatalytic water hydrogen production industry. Therefore, it is necessary to focus on exploring cost-effective alternative materials [5–7]. Whole-carbon materials, such as graphene and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [8], serve as cost-effective catalysts with great potential for hydrogen production.

CQDs, with sizes less than 10 nm, are zero-dimensional materials that have attracted interest for their electronic activities, chemical inertness, good solubility, and ease of surface modification [9,10]. Plenty of surface functional groups (-OH) provide more favorable anchors and active points for the preparation of multi-component and high-performance composites [11]. The doping of nitrogen atoms in nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) can adjust the electronic properties of adjacent carbon atoms through intramolecular charge transfer, promoting the performance of the HER. Therefore, N-CQDs exhibited satisfactory and stable catalytic activity for HER reaction [12]. At present, different types of raw materials and methods are used for the synthesis of CQDs. Monikankana Saikia et al. have employed hydrothermal technology to synthesize nanostructured carbon products CQDs and graphene nanosheets from carbon sources such as coal, petroleum coke, graphite, and coal-based humic acid [10]. CQDs can also be prepared using biomass (i.e. wheat straw, soybean and rice husk) as carbon sources [13,14]. Vinegar residue is a by-product produced by the vinegar manufacturing industry, mainly composing of by-products of vinegar fermentation and biomass such as rice bran, bran and sorghum shell [15]. China’s annual output of vinegar residue is huge. Its disposal methods are mainly divided into landfill and incineration treatment. However, due to its high acidity and water content, the improper disposal of vinegar residue can cause air and groundwater pollution, and even increase the risk of soil acidification [16]. Vinegar residue as carbon source to prepare CQDs can effectively treat environmental pollutants to reduce the impact on the environment, and can reduce the preparation cost of water-splitting catalysts. Due to the relatively poor electrical conductivity of CQDs, loading CQDs onto a suitable substrate may achieve better electrocatalytic effects [17]. The research of our group has proved that the vertically aligned graphene array (VG) has good vertical structure and mechanical strength, therefore it’s a suitable self-supporting carrier material [18]. Based on VG, our research group further has investigated nitrogen-doped vertically aligned graphene array (N-VG) and has found that N-VG improved the electrical properties of graphene by changing the band gap energy, which can be deemed as a good self-supporting electrode material [19]. Therefore, N-VG is considered as a substrate supporting N-CQDs, with good conductivity and high catalytic activity, which can effectively avoid CQDs agglomeration and expose more active sites for better electrocatalytic performance.

Here, we report a novel whole-carbon high-efficiency electrocatalyst (N-VG-CQDs), which loads CQDs based on vinegar residue onto VG substrates and introduces N atoms into VG and CQDs via a one-step doping method. The VG can be used as good carriers to improve the electrical conductivity and effectively prevent the aggregation of CQDs, and the introduction of N atoms can generate more active sites. N-VG-CQDs is expected to provide a reference for technologies in the preparation of clean and sustainable materials, and contribute to the development of the water splitting industry.

Sodium nitrate (NaNO3), the powder of graphite with a purity of 99.99 % (45 µm), hydrochloric acid (HCl) with a concentration of 37.5 %, sulphuric acid (H2SO4) with a concentration of 98 %, potassium hydroxide (KOH), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) 30 %, ethanol and isopropanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Commercial catalysts (IrO2 and 20 % Pt/C), as well as nafion perfluorinated resin solution (5 wt %), were produced by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Electric Co., Ltd. The vinegar residue used in the experiment was brought from GuoJunqi Sauce and Vinegar Factory in NanXiaozhang village. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) pipes (40 mm of inner diameter), whose thickness is 2 mm, were produced by Yangzhong Hongda Fluoroplastics Co., Ltd. Teflon tubes and copper plates made up the mold for directional freezing. Copper molds (44 mm of inner diameter and 10 mm of depth) were produced by Zhuji Yishun Building Materials Co., Ltd. Deionized (DI) water serves as a fundamental solvent in all experimental operations to keep the accuracy of the results.

Referring to the modified Hummer’s method, GO was prepared as the following steps [20]. First, 46 mL concentrated sulfuric acid was mixed with 1 g graphite powder and 1 g NaNO3 under an ice bath, followed by stirring for 20 min. KMnO4 (8 g) was then added carefully within 2 h. After the solution turned to yellow-green color, the solution was kept at 40 ℃ for 3 h to generate a dark-brown paste. 100 mL deionized water and 12 mL 30 % H2O2 were carefully added to the paste respectively. Then, the product was washed with HCl and deionized water several times by centrifugation, respectively. After dried in a 60 ℃ oven, the GO product was ready for the following experiment.

First, 10 mL of GO suspension (12 mg mL−1) was mixed with 30 mg urea and placed in a 25 mL centrifuge tube for ultrasonic treatment for 1 h, followed by adding 300 μL ethanol and another ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. Adding appropriate amount of ethanol as antifreeze could affect the growth shape and dynamics of ice crystals formation, thus helping to achieve the sublimation of ice crystals, forming the satisfactory vertical array structure [21]. After transferring the suspension into a mold assembled from a Teflon cylinder and copper plate, the mold was incompletely submerged in a plate filled with liquid nitrogen for 3–5 min. The samples were freeze-dried for 48 h and then annealed in a tube furnace to obtain N-VG. The tubular furnace was heated to 800 °C at a rate of 10 °C min−1 and was held in argon for 3 h. Finally, the tube furnace was slowly cooled under an argon atmosphere. A similar method was used to prepare VG (without the addition of urea) for later comparison with other materials.

First, 5 mg of catalyst (20 % Pt/C or IrO2) was weighed and 90 μL deionized (DI) water was pipetted into a 2 mL centrifuge tube. Then, after pipetting 900 μL of isopropanol and 10 μL of Nafion reagent into a centrifuge tube, the suspension was treated by ultrasound under an ice bath for 10–15 min to obtain a catalyst ink. The ink of Pt/C or IrO2 was slowly dropped on the surface of VG, and the sample was dried at room temperature to obtain VG-Pt/C or VG-IrO2.

Part of the vinegar residue was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 2 h, and then carbonized in a muffle furnace for 6 h at 400 °C. The carbonized products were ground into carbonized vinegar residue powder. The prepared carbonized vinegar residue powder was mixed with water at the ratio of 0.1 g: 10 mL to form a mixture. The mixture was placed in a reaction still for hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C and 8 h to obtain the hydrothermal reaction products. The obtained hydrothermal reaction products were filtered through a filter paper with an aperture of 100–500 nm to obtain the carbon quantum dot solution, and the CQDs were in the carbon quantum dot solution.

The GO prepared in Section 2.2 was configured into a solution with a concentration of 12 mg mL−1, the CQDs solution prepared in Section 2.5 and the urea mixed up in a ratio of 5 mL: 5 mL: 30 mg, and after 1 h ultrasonication to form a mixed solution. The mixed solution was freeze-dried in a cold drying machine for 48 h to obtain graphene and CQD aerogel. The aerogel was placed in a tube furnace and after high-temperature calcination at 800 °C min−1 for 3 h, the N-VG-CQDs electrocatalyst was obtained. The synthesis process of N-VG-CQDs is shown in Scheme 1.

Scanning electronic microscope (SEM) images were obtained by using a Zeiss Gemini 500 field emission SEM (Zeiss Co., Germany). The morphology of N-VG-CQDs was analyzed by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, ZEISS EVO10). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on the Cu Kα radiation in Bragg-Brentano (θ-2θ) configuration. Raman spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB Xi+) were performed for the characterization of the materials.

The electrochemical activity of the electrocatalysts were tested via the CHI660E workstation (CH instrument, Shanghai ChenhuaCo., China). All electrochemical tests were performed at ambient temperature. Electrochemical testing was carried out in a standard three-electrode system, with the prepared electrocatalyst serving as a working electrode, the carbon rod as a counter electrode, calomel electrodes as reference electrode. On the basis of referring to a large number of literature surveys and studies, 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 were selected as electrolytes for electrochemical performance testing [22–24]. The correction steps were as follows: First the 1.0 M KOH electrolyte was prepared, and then the reference electrodes to be tested and a new reference electrode were clamped on both sides of the digital voltmeter and were placed in the prepared electrolyte. When the electrode potential observed was stable within 5 mV, it meant that the potential of the reference electrode was stable and then was used for the subsequent electrochemical properties test. Working electrodes were always tested at a stable level. The scan rate of linear scanning voltammetry (LSV) was always at 5 mV s−1. The measured LSV curve potential was converted into the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) using the following equation:

where the η is the overpotential, j is the current density, and a and b are the Tafel constant and Tafel slope, respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) with scan rates ranging from 5 to 200 mV−1 was used to measure the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) in the non-Faradaic potential region, which can be used to evaluate the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of catalysts [25]. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was calculated by normalizing Cdl with the following formula:
(3)

Cdl
Cs
where Cs is 0.040 mF cm−2. The electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was measured by applying an AC voltage of 100 kHz to 0.001 Hz. Current-time response was monitored by the time-positive intensity measurement technique, and the stability of the catalyst was measured at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The Faraday efficiency (FE) was calculated from the following formula:
(4)

nV F
It

where V is the volume generating the gas (H2 or O2), n is the number of electrons transferred to the H2 or O2 molecules, F is the Faraday constant (96,485 C mol−1), I is the current measured during the experiment, t is the recorded time, and Vm is the molar volume at 25 °C and 1.01 × 10^5 Pa (24.5 L mol−1).

All the Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed by the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP). The electrons exchange correlation function and the interactions between the nuclei and electrons were described by Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) and projector augmented wave (PAW) pseudo-potentials, respectively. The electron’s wave function expanded on a plane wave basis with a cutoff energy of 500 eV. The convergence units of energy and force in the process of structural relaxation were respectively set as 10−5 and 10−4. During the calculations, a 3 × 3 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-points grid was used. To avoid the effects of periodic structures, all computational models added the 15 Å vacuum layer vertically and horizontally. VG and CQDs were simulated via building a double layer of C atoms, and CQDs were added to the center of the upper layer of the VG in the original model.

SEM, TEM, Fluorescence spectra of synthesized N-CQDs aqueous solution at 365 nm UV light, UV-Vis absorption spectrum, HRTEM, XRD, Raman, Pore size distribution and the mercury intrusion and extrusion curve, EDS and XPS analysis of N-VG-CQDs electrocatalyst.

The SEM of the VG is characterized by a distinct vertical structure ( Fig. 1a). The TEM of the N-VG-CQDs is shown in 1b, showing that the CQDs are distributed on the VG substrate with a particle size of about 10 nm. The CQDs are successfully loaded onto the vertical array graphene substrate (Fig. 1a and b). The fluorescence of N-CQDs under 365 nm UV irradiation shows that the N-CQDs aqueous solution displays a bright blue fluorescence effect under UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 1c), which is consistent with the phenomena described by the literature [26]. The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1d. The N-CQDs aqueous solution displays a strong ultraviolet absorption peak at about 234 nm. The HRTEM image of N-VG-CQDs shows that the corresponding lattice spacings of graphene and CQDs are 0.34 nm and 0.22 nm, respectively (Fig. 1e), which is in good agreement with the results in the literature [27]. The XRD of N-VG-CQDs shows that the diffraction peaks at 26.2° and 45.6° correspond to the (002) plane and (101) plane of graphitic carbon (PDF#41-1487) (Fig. 1f). According to the 002 (0.338 nm) plane and the 101 (0.204 nm) plane shown on the card, the lattice spacings corresponding to the card are in good agreement with the HRTEM results. The EDS plots of N-VG-CQDs are shown in Fig. 1g–i. The catalyst mainly contains three elements, C, O, and N, which are uniformly distributed on the catalyst. These figures show that N atoms are successfully doped into VG and CQDs. XPS is used to characterize the chemical states of N-VG-CQDs catalyst. The measured spectra (Fig. 1j and k) show the presence of C, O and N, which is consistent with the results shown in the EDS image, indicating that the N atoms have been successfully doped. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 1j shows three peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.2 eV and 286.6 eV, which are related to graphitic CC, C-N and CO bindings, respectively [28]. The peak of C-N in C 1s indicates that the nitrogen atoms in N-doped graphene can be formed by high-temperature annealing of urea-containing graphene. The CO peak in C 1s indicates that the oxygenated functional groups are not completely removed after high-temperature annealing treatment, consistent with the EDS characterization. The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 1k shows three peaks at 398.4 eV, 399.3 eV and 400.9 eV, which are related to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N bindings, respectively [28]. Pyridine N is more abundant than the other two nitrogen forms, and pyridine N and pyrrolic N are the main nitrogen forms. The structural changes in VG, N-VG and N-VG-CQDs are detected by Raman spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 1l. The D peaks at 1327 cm−1 and the G peaks at 1586 cm−1 correspond to the disordered and plane vibrations of the carbon atoms, respectively. In general, the D peak in the Raman spectra represents defects in the sample lattice, and the intensity ratio to the G peak (ID/IG) reflects the disorder of the carbon materials [29]. The ID/IG ratios of VG, N-VG and N-VG-CQDs are 1.01, 1.12 and 1.14, respectively. The introduction of nitrogen on the VG substrate increases the ID/IG, which suggests that N doping introduces more disordered structures and surface defects to VG. Due to the numerous defects in the N-CQDs surface itself, loading the N-CQDs onto the N-VG substrate further increases the number of defect sites on the N-VG substrate, allowing the N-VG-CQDs to exhibit better electrocatalytic properties [30]. Fig. 1m-o are the pore distribution diagram of VG, N-VG, N-VG-CQDs. From the figures, it can be seen that the pore size of the VG is about 10–1000 nm, and the pore size of N-VG and N-VG-CQDs is about 100–1000 nm. The macropore promotes the penetration of electrolyte solution and the release of gas [31]. Fig. 1p-r are the mercury intrusion and extrusion curve of the VG, N-VG and N-VG-CQDs, respectively. As can be seen from the figures, their total mercury intrusion volume are 62.21 mL/g, 83.71 mL/g, 96.41 mL/g, and their specific surface areas are 40.9 m2/g, 60.1 m2/g, and 66.8 m2/g, respectively. It can be seen that the introduction of N and N-CQDs increases the specific surface area of VG, making the N-VG-CQDs has a larger specific surface area. The larger specific surface area of N-VG-CQDs is conducive to the contact between catalyst and electrolyte solution, and promotes the occurrence of the water splitting reaction.

Fig. 1 shows that we’ve successfully prepared a graphene substrate of a vertical structure and loaded the prepared CQDs on the substrate, then introduced N atoms into the VG and CQDs. The catalyst has large pores and large specific surface area, which can effectively promote water splitting and gas release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical property of the prepared electrocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: A large number of working electrodes are tested, and the test results maintain good electrochemical performance and similarity, indicating that the catalysts have a reliable performance. The activities of the electrocatalysts toward the HER in both alkaline and acidic electrolyte solutions are shown in Fig. 2a and c. The HER activity in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 is ranked as VG < N-VG < N-VG-CQDs < VG-Pt/C. N-VG-CQDs exhibits better HER activity than VG and N-VG, and requires a low overpotential of 218 mV and 238 mV in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolytic solutions, respectively, to drive the current density of 10 mA cm−2. Fig. 2b and d show that the Tafel slopes of N-VG-CQDs in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolytic solutions are 71 mV dec−1 and 103 mV dec−1, respectively. According to the Tafel values of N-VG-CQDs, it can be known that the mechanism of HER in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte is Volmer-Heyrovsky (M+H2O+e-→M-Had +OH- and M-Had+H2O+e-→M+OH-+H2) [32]. Among these reactions, Had are H atoms adsorbed to the active sites of electrocatalyst.

The activities of the electrocatalysts toward the OER in both alkaline and acidic electrolyte solutions are shown in Fig. 2e and g. The electrocatalytic performance of electrocatalysts towards OER in 1 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte solutions is ranked as VG-IrO2 > N-VG-CQDs > N-VG > VG. VG-IrO2 shows the best electrocatalytic activity, requiring an overpotential of 333 mV and 285 mV in alkaline and acid electrolyte solutions to reach the 10 mA cm−2 of current density, respectively. In alkaline and acidic electrolyte solutions, N-VG-CQDs displays an overpotential of 383 mV and 497 mV, respectively, and the Tafel slopes of 175 mV dec−1 and 373 mV dec−1, respectively (Fig. 2e–h). According to the Tafel values of N-VG-CQDs, it can be known that the mechanism of OER in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte solutions is Volmer-Heyrovsky (M+H2O+e-→M-Had+ OH- and M-Had+H2O+e-→M+OH-+H2). Among these reactions, Had are H atoms adsorbed to the active sites of electrocatalyst. The results of the above-mentioned multiple electrochemical tests show that the electrochemical performance of the catalysts we tested have excellent reproducibility. Compared with the VG and N-VG, N-VG-CQDs exhibits the best catalytic activity. These results suggest that N-CQDs can promote the HER and OER processes. Meanwhile, N-VG is an ideal carrier for dispersing the N-CQDs. Therefore, N-VG-CQDs acts as a good whole-carbon electrocatalyst for overall water splitting in a wide range of pH and exhibits better electrochemical activity under alkaline conditions. This may be due to more defects produced upon loading of N-CQDs on graphene, which can enable adsorption of H+ and promote gas release. The introduction of nitrogen and N-CQDs increases the active sites on the VG surface and enables good electrochemical properties.

The electrochemical performance of N-VG-CQDs is compared with recently reported metal-based electrocatalysts and non-metal-based electrocatalysts in 1.0 M KOH electrolyte at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. The N-VG-CQDs has the similar HER ( Fig. 3a) and OER (Fig. 3b) performance and the lower preparation cost compared to other metal-based catalysts [33–40]. Compared with other non-metal-based electrocatalysts, N-VG-CQDs exhibits excellent HER (Fig. 3c) and OER (Fig. 3d) performance and is a more environmentally friendly material [41–48]. In summary, N-VG-CQDs has great potential for industrial application because of their good electrochemical performance, wide source, low price and less environmental pollution. This work is of great significance for reducing environmental pollution and seeking low-cost catalysts for water splitting.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical surface area (ECSA) test, EIS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) tends to be proportional to the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the catalyst and thus can be used to evaluate the ECSA value of the catalyst. The Cdl values of VG, N-VG and N-VG-CQDs in alkaline solution are shown in Fig. 4a. The Cdl values of VG, N-VG and N-VG-CQDs are 0.34 mF cm−2, 1.26 mF cm−2 and 4.74 mF cm−2, respectively. Therefore, N-VG-CQDs possesses the largest ECSA in 1.0 M KOH, which is calculated to be 118.5 cm2, and the ECSA of VG and N-VG are 8.5 cm2 and 31.5 cm2, respectively. A larger value of ECSA means that more catalytically active sites are exposed on the catalyst surface, which is beneficial to increase the HER and OER reaction rates [49]. Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) is used to research dynamics at the electrode/electrolyte interface in the electrical catalytic process. Fig. 4b is the electrochemical impedance spectrum of three catalysts, where the illustration is an equivalent circuit diagram. The equivalent circuit diagram consists of three parts: solution resistance (Rs), constant phase component (CPE) and charge transfer resistance (Rct). Rs represents the resistance between the electrode and the electrolyte interface, including ion resistance and electronic resistance, the inherent resistance of the electrode and the contact resistance of the electrode/current collector, and it corresponds to the initial value of semi-circular [50–52]. Rct represents the resistance encountered during the charge transfer process based on the oxidation reaction, corresponding to the diameter of the semicircular. The Rct of VG, N-VG and N-VG-CQDs are shown to be 14.2 Ω, 10.1 Ω and 5.8 Ω, respectively. N-VG-CQDs has the smallest charge transfer resistance, so it has a high conductivity. The Cdl of N-VG-CQDs is greater than VG and N-VG, indicating that the introduction of N atoms and CQDs on the graphene substrate increases the degree of defects in the graphene substrate. The low impedance of N-VG-CQDs suggests the fastest reaction kinetics during HER and OER process, which may be due to the well-dispersed N-CQDs and the higher intrinsic activity of the vertically aligned structure of graphene. Overall, N-VG-CQDs exhibits better electronic and ionic conductivity and better charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS analysis after HER and OER, Free energy diagram of HER and Schematic diagram of water splitting of N-VG-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: XPS is conducted on N-VG-CQDs after HER and OER stability measurement ( Fig. 5a and b). The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 5a shows three peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.2 eV and 286.3 eV after the HER process, which are related to graphitic CC, C-N and CO bindings, respectively. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 5a shows three peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.2 eV and 286.6 eV after the OER process, which are related to graphitic CC, C-N and CO bindings, respectively. After the HER and OER process, the position of the peak of CC, C-N, and CO is no obviously changed, indicating that the electrocatalyst is relatively stable during the HER and OER process [28]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 5b) can be deconvolved into three nitrogen peaks, at 398.1 eV, 399.6 eV and 400.9 eV after the HER process. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 5b) can be deconvolved into three nitrogen peaks, at 398.4 eV, 399.3 eV and 400.9 eV after the OER process [53]. In the HER process, the nitrogen forms dominated by pyridinic N and pyrrolic N are combined with H, and the adjacent carbon are effectively activated to facilitate the HER reaction [44]. In the OER process, the nitrogen forms dominated by pyridine N and pyrrolic N bind with intermediates such as O*, OH*, and OOH*, thereby enhancing the electrocatalytic activity and promoting the release of O2 [54]. Therefore, the higher the content of pyridine N and pyrrolic N, the better the electrochemical performance of the catalyst [55]. After the HER and OER reactions, the relative contents of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N remain high (Fig. 5b), enabling the catalyst to exhibit excellent electrocatalytic performance.

To investigate the interaction of N-VG and N-CQDs, DFT calculations are performed. ΔG(*-H) being close to zero indicates that H atoms are not only easily absorbed on the catalyst surface, but can also desorb and release H2 molecules [56,57]. ΔG (*-H) are calculated for VG, N-VG and N-VG-CQDs, respectively. In Fig. 5c, the DFT calculation results show that N-VG-CQDs has the best HER performance, which is consistent with the results in Fig. 2a and c. The HER performance of N-VG is better than that of VG, which may be due to the redistribution of graphene surface charge caused by N doping, thereby optimizing the adsorption energy of hydrogen intermediates and improving the intrinsic catalytic activity of graphene. The HER performance of N-VG-CQDs is better than N-VG, indicating that loading N-CQDs onto N-VG has better HER performance, which possibly due to more defects on the surface of N-CQDs. N-VG as a substrate can effectively prevent N-CQDs agglomeration, so that the N-VG-CQDs can adsorb hydrogen intermediates and release more H2. Our results show that N-VG and N-CQDs can promote each other, making N-VG-CQDs exhibits the best electrochemical performance. Therefore, N-VG-CQDs exerts optimal adsorption and desorption effects on the reaction intermediates. The DFT calculation results further explain the best electrochemical performance of N-VG-CQDs. Fig. 5d is a schematic diagram of N-VG-CQDs during HER and OER reactions. It can be seen that the introduction of N on the VG increases the defects on the surface of VG. And the introduction of N-CQDs further increase the active sites on the N-VG surface. Compared with VG, N-VG-CQDs has more active sites, which can adsorb more H+ and OH-, thereby releasing more H2 and O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM and TEM after HER and OER stability test of N-VG-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 is SEM and TEM after the HER and OER stability test. The figures show that after a long-term HER (Fig. 6a) and OER (Fig. 6b) stability test, the N-VG-CQDs still maintains an excellent vertical structure. The figures show that after a long-term HER (Fig. 6c) and OER (Fig. 6d) stability test, CQDs are still scattered on the graphene base. The above results show that compared with the appearance before the test, the structure and appearance of the N-VG-CQDs catalyst remain well after a long-term stability test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical stability and Faraday efficiency test
ce:section.ce:para: The HER ( Fig. 7a) and OER (Fig. 7b) stabilities of N-VG-CQDs electrocatalyst in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4 electrolyte solutions are tested at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, respectively. No obvious change of overpotential of N-VG-CQDs can be observed in both acidic or alkaline electrolytes after a long-term stability test of 9000 s, suggesting the remarkable stability of N-VG-CQDs catalyst as a self-supported bifunctional electrocatalyst in a wide pH range. Faraday efficiency refers to the percentage of actual collection of products and theoretical products, and the experimental volumes of H2 and O2 are collected by the water-gas displacing instrument. In Figure, the N-VG-CQDs is simultaneously worked as the cathode and anode for overall water splitting to generate H2 and O2. The cell composed of N-VG-CQDs electrodes require a voltage of 1.85 V (Fig. 7c) and 1.97 V (Fig. 7d) to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH and 0.5 M H2SO4, respectively. And bubbles are continuously generated on the surface of the electrodes during the test, indicating that the N-VG-CQDs has excellent electrocatalytic capabilities [58]. Faraday efficiency (FE) of N-VG-CQDs in Fig. 7e and f indicates its importance for estimating the practical application potential of electrocatalysts towards overall water splitting. Fig. 7e shows a linear relationship between the amount and time of the gas collected. Based on the ratio obtained to theoretical gases, the Faraday efficiency of hydrogen production and oxygen production are 83.3 % and 81.4 %, respectively (Fig. 7f), showing excellent application potential. The good stability and the high Faraday efficiency of N-VG-CQDs prove it a well whole-carbon water-splitting catalyst with excellent utility, which is expected to become an effective alternative to precious metal electrolytic water catalysts and is widely used in industrial water-splitting.

In conclusion, CQDs are successfully prepared with vinegar residue waste as carbon source, and a whole-carbon splitting catalyst (N-VG-CQDs) is successfully synthesized by introducing N onto CQDs and VG. The good performance of N-VG-CQDs may mainly come from three aspects, including the large specific surface area brought by the unique structure of VG and CQDs, more active sites brought by N doping, and the interaction between N-VG and N-CQDs. In addition to its high catalytic activity, N-VG-CQDs also exhibits good stability and high Faradaic efficiency, indicating a potential for large-scale industrial applications. Compared with metal catalysts, N-VG-CQDs has similar electrochemical performance and lower preparation cost. Compared with non-metal catalysts, N-VG-CQDs has good electrochemical performance and less environmental pollution. Therefore, N-VG-CQDs has the advantages of excellent electrochemical performance, good stability, low cost, and is a potential electrocatalyst with less pollution. The study provides an effective reference for preparing electrocatalyst for dual-functional whole-carbon materials with good catalytic activity, which is invaluable for reducing waste disposal costs and protecting the environment. This work also broadens the idea of using more environmental pollutants to prepare water splitting catalysts, which in turn promotes the development of the water splitting industry.
